## What this is

Your family member has been diagnosed with dementia. The appointment is over, and you are carrying a word you did not choose. This module covers the first two weeks — what is normal to feel, what can wait, and the small number of things worth doing now.

<!-- provenance: composite -->
Many caregivers describe a strange split in the hours after the appointment: part of them is relieved that there is finally a name for what has been happening, and part of them is grieving a future that just quietly closed. The person beside them in the car is telling the same joke they told this morning. Nothing visible has changed. But the mental list of things they assumed would happen — the retirement trip, the years of sharp conversation, the growing old on equal footing — has rearranged itself without asking permission. Holding both of those truths at the same time is not a sign of confusion. It is an accurate reading of the situation.

How the diagnosis was made may look different from what you expected. The workup usually includes a history, a cognitive screen (often the MoCA, MMSE, or Mini-Cog), bloodwork (thyroid, B12), and imaging (usually MRI). In 2025–2026, many clinicians also use a blood biomarker test — measuring phosphorylated tau — as part of the workup [1][2][3]. As of 2025, two are FDA-cleared: the Fujirebio Lumipulse pTau217/Aβ42 ratio [2] and the Roche Elecsys pTau181 [3]. The Alzheimer's Association published its first clinical guideline on these tests in July 2025, reporting that they agree with the traditional PET brain scan 91.7% of the time when positive and 97.3% when negative [1]. Your doctor may have used one of these blood tests, a PET scan, a spinal tap, or a combination. The method does not change what to do this week — and a separate module covers the diagnostic pathway in depth.

## Why it matters now

The first two weeks are when caregivers are most likely to make decisions they later regret — selling a house, quitting a job (you may have FMLA leave available — do not resign before checking [8]), telling everyone at once — or to freeze and do nothing, including things that genuinely matter [5][7]. The 2024 Lancet Commission noted that timely support immediately following diagnosis reduces long-term caregiver distress [7]. This is not a crisis. It is a transition, and transitions have a shape.

<!-- provenance: composite -->
It is common for caregivers in the first few days to feel a pull toward action — researching facilities at 2 a.m., reorganizing the kitchen for safety, drafting emails to every sibling. The impulse makes sense: if the future is uncertain, doing something feels like control. But many find, looking back, that the decisions they made in the first seventy-two hours were the ones they most wanted to undo. Not because the decisions were wrong, but because they were made on a nervous system that had not yet steadied. The things that helped most, it turns out, were small and boring: writing down what the doctor said, scheduling the follow-up, going to bed.

Most of what feels urgent right now does not have to be solved this week. See the **Things That Can Wait** checklist for the full list, but the short version: long-term financial planning, choosing a memory-care community, telling extended family in a particular order, changing the will, selling the house, big arguments about who should do what. None of these get worse if you take ten days to breathe first.

## What to do this week

Pick one or two of these. Not all of them. The **First Two Weeks Action Items** checklist has the details.

**Write down what the clinician said** while you still remember: the type of dementia named (or "not yet specified"), any medications started, what tests are pending, and when the follow-up is (usually 2–4 weeks) [5]. If you did not get clear answers, send a message to the clinic. You are allowed to ask twice.

**Tell one person.** Not the whole family, not a group text. One person you trust who can sit with the news without making it about themselves [6]. You do not need a script — but if you need an opening: "I need to tell you something about Mom" or "We got some news at the doctor, and I need to say it out loud to someone." If there is no one safe in your life right now, the Alzheimer's Association 24/7 Helpline (1-800-272-3900) is staffed by licensed clinicians [5].

<!-- provenance: composite -->
Caregivers often describe the moment of telling someone as harder than the appointment itself. There is the question of who — not the person who will panic, not the one who will minimize, but the one who can just hear it. Many say they did not plan what to say. They called someone, started with something ordinary, and then it came out. The conversation did not fix anything. But afterward, the weight was in two bodies instead of one, and that turned out to matter more than any advice.

**Ask the clinic if they participate in the GUIDE program.** The CMS GUIDE Model (Guiding an Improved Dementia Experience) went live at 390 organizations in July 2025 [4]. If your clinic participates, you may be eligible for care navigation, caregiver education, and respite support — reimbursed up to roughly $2,500 per year (as of 2025) [4]. One phone call can tell you. You can also check at cms.gov/guide.

**Locate key documents.** Find the most recent driver's license or state ID, health insurance cards (including Medicare or Medicaid cards), and any existing power of attorney or healthcare proxy / advance directive. You do not need to act on them. You just need to know where they are. If they do not exist yet, that is a month-two task [5].

**Know that treatment options may come up.** Your clinician may mention disease-modifying therapies that did not exist a few years ago [7]. That is a month-one conversation between you, your family member, and the clinical team — not a week-one decision.

**Eat something. Sleep, if you can.** The body keeps the score, and you are going to need it [6].

## When to call sooner

Some things in the first two weeks warrant a call back to the clinician — do not wait for the next appointment.

- **Sudden increased confusion over hours or days.** A noticeable jump in confusion can signal delirium — look for fever, sudden confusion markedly worse than baseline, or reduced alertness. A common trigger is a urinary tract infection [7]. Delirium is not the dementia getting worse overnight; it is a medical emergency layered on top, and it is treatable when caught early.
- **New medication side effects.** If a medication was started and your family member develops nausea, dizziness, falls, or new agitation — call [5][6].
- **Safety concerns today.** If driving feels unsafe right now, call the clinic; they have a protocol for this [5]. If your family member is wandering or leaving the stove on, call.
- **Talk of self-harm or harm to others.** Call the clinic, or contact emergency services. That is not an overreaction [5][6].

If **you** are the one not okay — if you are having thoughts of hurting yourself, or you cannot keep yourself or your family member safe — contact emergency services or call/text 988 (Suicide & Crisis Lifeline). That is the system working as intended.

## How to care for yourself in this season

This is grief, even though no one has died. The person you love is still here, and a particular version of the future is not. Both of those are real at the same time, and that doubleness is what makes the early weeks so disorienting [7]. You do not have to name it as grief out loud yet if that word feels too big. You can call it tired. You can call it strange. You can call it nothing.

<!-- provenance: composite -->
What is rarely said out loud is that the grief of the first two weeks feels illegitimate — no one has died, the person is right there, and yet something enormous has been lost. Caregivers often describe being embarrassed by what actually helps: not therapy or a support group (those come later, and they help), but canceling one plan, eating a meal sitting down, walking to the end of the block and back. The smallness of it feels like it should not be enough. But in a week when the nervous system is running on adrenaline and bad sleep, lowering the bar is not giving up. It is the first real thing most caregivers do for themselves.

The Lancet Commission found that even minimal early intervention — a single counseling session, a peer-support contact, a structured information packet — reduced longer-term caregiver distress [7]. You do not need to sign up for a support group tonight. But the Alzheimer's Association (1-800-272-3900) [5] and the National Institute on Aging's ADEAR Center (1-800-438-4380) [6] both maintain free caregiver resource centers designed for exactly this moment.

Do not make any decisions you would not make on a regular Tuesday. The ground will steady, slowly, and when it does you will think more clearly than you can right now. The next module is there when you are ready, and not before.
